content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Contextualized Prompt Filtering for Robust Language Model Defense",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) remain susceptible to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attacks that subvert system instructions or bypass safety guardrails. Existing static prompting or fine-tuning approaches are insufficient against novel or adaptive attack strategies.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes and publicly accessible environments, their vulnerability to prompt-based attacks poses serious risks, including misinformation and harmful outputs. Improving robustness at the prompt layer allows for lightweight, updatable defenses without the resource cost of full retraining.",
  "method": "We propose a dynamic contextualized prompt filtering mechanism: before executing a user prompt, an auxiliary LLM-based classifier analyzes both the user and system prompts, as well as recent conversation context, to predict the likelihood of adversarial intent or jailbreak attempts. If flagged as high-risk, the original prompt is automatically rewritten using a controlled paraphrasing module to remove or mask suspicious linguistic patterns, while preserving benign intent. The response is then generated from this filtered prompt, minimizing attack surface without overblocking.",
  "experiment_plan": "Test on AdvBench, jailbreak prompt datasets (e.g., JailbreakEval), and standard safety benchmarks. Compare against baseline LLMs, static prompt filtering, and defense-tuned models. Metrics include attack success rate, safety/harms detected, benign utility retention, and false positive rates."
}
